title: CPQ Exercises — Pricing & Quoting
description: Hands-on exercises covering pricebooks, discounts, promotions, quote workflow, approval rules, custom fields, and document generation.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Multi-Market Pricebook Setup
sections.content:
Objective

Set up a complete pricing structure with currencies, markets, and a pricebook hierarchy that demonstrates parent/child price fallback.

Scenario

Your company sells networking equipment in two markets: Europe (EUR) and USA (USD). Gold-tier partners get special pricing; standard customers use the base pricebook.

Steps

Part A — Currencies & Markets

  1. Navigate to Setup → Pricing/Calculations → Currencies. Verify EUR is the default currency. Add USD with a conversion rate of 1.10.
  2. Navigate to Setup → Pricing/Calculations → Markets. Create two markets:
    • YourInitials_Europe — Currency: EUR, Market Factor: 1
    • YourInitials_USA — Currency: USD, Market Factor: 1
  3. Set market visibility so both are visible to your company and user type.

Part B — Pricebook Hierarchy

  1. Navigate to Setup → Pricing/Calculations → Pricebooks. Select the Europe market.
  2. Create a parent pricebook: YourInitials_EU_Standard. Set Visible to Everyone. Add entries for your Router products:
    • RTR100: Price 149, Cost 90
    • RTR200: Price 299, Cost 180
    • RTR300: Price 599, Cost 350
  3. Create a child pricebook: YourInitials_EU_Gold_Partner with parent = EU_Standard. Add only one entry:
    • RTR300: Price 499, Cost 350 (Gold partners get the Enterprise router cheaper)
  4. Repeat for the USA market with USD prices (multiply EUR prices by 1.1 and round).

Part C — Test the Fallback

  1. Set your user's default pricebook to YourInitials_EU_Gold_Partner.
  2. Create a new quote. Add all three router products.
  3. Verify: RTR100 = 149 (from parent, no Gold override), RTR200 = 299 (from parent), RTR300 = 499 (from Gold child pricebook, overriding the parent's 599).
  4. Switch to the Standard pricebook and verify RTR300 shows 599.

Validation

The fallback mechanism should be clear: the child pricebook only overrides RTR300; RTR100 and RTR200 fall back to the parent. Switch between markets and verify currency conversion applies correctly.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Quote Workflow with Custom Status & Notifications
sections.content:
Objective

Design and implement a custom quote workflow with a new status, transitional and non-transitional actions, required fields, and an email notification.

Scenario

Your company needs a "Technical Review" status between Preparing and Submitted — the sales rep sends the quote for technical validation before it goes to the customer.

Steps

Part A — Create the Status

  1. Navigate to Setup → Workflow/Approval → Statuses.
  2. Create a new status: YourInitials_Technical_Review, Rank: 500.

Part B — Configure Workflow Actions

  1. Navigate to Setup → Workflow/Approval → Workflow, My Quotes tab.
  2. Add a transitional action "Request Tech Review" from Preparing → YourInitials_Technical_Review.
  3. From YourInitials_Technical_Review, add:
    • Non-transitional actions: View, Edit, Save Quote, Add Item.
    • Transitional action: "Tech Review Approved" → back to Preparing (so the rep can then submit normally).
    • Transitional action: "Tech Review Rejected" → back to Preparing (with comments for revision).
  4. Set permissions: grant all actions to your SalesManager user type.
  5. Set a required field: before "Request Tech Review" can execute, the Sold-To Party must be filled.

Part C — Add a Notification

  1. Navigate to Setup → Workflow/Approval → Notifications.
  2. Create an email list that includes your email address.
  3. Create a notification with a message: Subject = "Quote {{quote number}} needs technical review", Body = "A quote has been submitted for technical review. Click the link below to review it." Use Formula Builder variables for the quote number and link.
  4. Attach this notification to the "Request Tech Review" workflow action.

Validation

Create a quote, add products, fill in the Sold-To Party, and execute "Request Tech Review". Verify: the status changes, an email is sent to your address, the available actions are limited to what you configured, and you can transition back to Preparing via either approval or rejection action.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Approval Rules with Discount Thresholds
sections.content:
Objective

Implement a discount-driven approval flow where sales reps can discount up to 10% freely, 10–20% requires manager approval, and above 20% requires director approval.

Steps

Part A — Discount Rules

  1. Navigate to Setup → Pricing/Calculations → Discounts.
  2. Create a discount rule for your SalesRep user type: Min 0%, Max 20%, Default 0%, Product: All. Priority: 1.
  3. Create a discount rule for your SalesManager user type: Min 0%, Max 30%, Default 0%, Product: All. Priority: 1.

Part B — Approval Rules

  1. Navigate to Setup → Workflow/Approval → Approval.
  2. Create Approval Rule 1: YourInitials_Manager_Approval.
    • Step 1: Apply to users of type SalesRep.
    • Step 2: Condition — discount percentage on any line item exceeds 10%.
  3. Create Approval Rule 2: YourInitials_Director_Approval.
    • Step 1: Apply to users of type SalesRep and SalesManager.
    • Step 2: Condition — discount percentage on any line item exceeds 20%.
  4. Optionally, create an Approval Branch with AND logic linking both rules — so that a 25% discount requires both manager AND director approval.

Part C — Test the Flow

  1. Log in as a SalesRep user. Create a quote and add a product.
  2. Apply a 5% discount — verify no approval is triggered.
  3. Change the discount to 15% — verify the approval rule triggers and Request Approval becomes available.
  4. Submit for approval. Log in as the SalesManager (approval parent) and verify the quote appears under Waiting for Approval.
  5. Approve the quote and verify the status changes.
  6. Repeat with a 25% discount and verify both approval levels are triggered.

Validation

The key learning: discount rules alone don't trigger approval — you need explicit approval rules. Verify each threshold boundary (exactly 10%, exactly 20%) and confirm the approval chain routes to the correct approver(s).

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Custom Fields, Quote Tables & Document Output
sections.content:
Objective

Extend the quote with custom fields on header and item level, create a quote table for additional data, and generate a PDF document that includes all this data.

Steps

Part A — Custom Quote Tab & Fields

  1. Create a custom quote tab: YourInitials_Project_Info (Rank: 500). Set permissions for your company.
  2. Create a Quote Custom Field (QCF): YourInitials_Project_Name, Type: Free Form, Tab: YourInitials_Project_Info, Order: 10. Set "Save Quote on Change" = checked. Set permissions: Editable in Preparing for your user type, Read Only in all other statuses.
  3. Create a second QCF: YourInitials_Expected_Close_Date, Type: Date, same tab, Order: 20.
  4. Create a Quote Item Custom Field (QICF): YourInitials_Item_Notes, Type: String. Make it editable in Preparing for your user type. Add it to the layout and make the column visible for your company.

Part B — Quote Table

  1. Create a Quote Table: Label = YourInitials Delivery Schedule, Name = YourInitials_Delivery_Schedule, Tab = Quotation, Rank: 200. Set permissions for your company.
  2. Add columns: Milestone (String, Editable), Date (Date, Editable), Complete (Boolean, Editable), Notes (String, Editable).
  3. Enable standard actions: Add Row, Delete Row.

Part C — Test the Full Quote

  1. Create a new quote. Fill in the Project Info tab with a project name and expected close date.
  2. Add products and fill in Item Notes for each line item.
  3. Navigate to the Quotation tab and use the Delivery Schedule table: add 3 rows with milestones (Kickoff, Phase 1 Delivery, Go-Live) and dates.
  4. Generate a document (PDF) using the Generate Documents action.
  5. Review the output: verify that the standard quote data appears, and note which custom data (QCFs, QICFs, quote table) appears in the output. If custom data is missing from the output, this indicates the document template needs to be updated with the appropriate tags — note this as a finding.

Validation

You should have a fully enriched quote with header custom fields, item custom fields, a quote table with multiple rows, and a generated PDF. Document which data appeared in the PDF and which didn't — this teaches the important lesson that custom data requires template tag configuration to appear in output.
